title: Account for viewport safe areas on mobile devices
summary: AI-generated mobile interfaces with fixed-position elements or full-bleed content fail to use CSS env(safe-area-inset-*) variables, placing interactive content behind device notches, rounded corners, and home indicators.
instruction: On mobile viewports with edge-to-edge content or fixed-position elements, use CSS env(safe-area-inset-*) variables to keep interactive content clear of device notches, rounded corners, and the home indicator. Apply safe-area padding to fixed navigation bars, bottom sheets, and full-bleed backgrounds.
rationale: The CSS Environment Variables specification defines env(safe-area-inset-*) as standard viewport-relative insets that adapt to each device's physical display shape. Without them, fixed-position bottom bars can sit behind the iOS home indicator, and full-bleed headers can have content obscured by the notch or status bar. The specification is supported in Safari since iOS 11 and in Chromium-based mobile browsers.

violations: A mobile navigation bar with position: fixed at the bottom, placed behind the iOS home indicator with no safe-area-inset-bottom padding.
A full-width hero image using background-size: cover that places critical text behind the device notch on iOS.
controls: A fixed bottom navigation with padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom, 16px) providing fallback-safe spacing.
A page layout applying padding-top: env(safe-area-inset-top, 0px) to the main content to clear the status bar area.
borderline: A landscape game or video player that intentionally uses the full screen including notch area for immersive content -- this should be an explicit opt-in.

exceptions.description: Immersive full-screen experiences (games, video playback) that intentionally use the entire display including safe areas, with explicit user opt-in.

detector.signalDescription: Search for CSS env(safe-area-inset-*) usage. Flag if fixed/sticky positioned elements exist at viewport edges without corresponding safe-area padding.
limitations: The actual safe-area values are device-specific and cannot be determined from static CSS analysis.
Some CSS frameworks handle safe areas implicitly through viewport meta tag settings.
Desktop browsers ignore env(safe-area-inset-*) so the absence may not be visible during development.

scenarios: Desktop-only interfaces where safe areas do not apply.
Frameworks that handle safe areas through a base stylesheet not visible to the linter.
Older mobile browsers that do not support env() variables.
mitigations: Only flag at mobile viewport breakpoints. Accept CSS with env() fallback values as good practice.

fixturePlan.description: A mobile page with fixed bottom navigation and no safe-area-inset-bottom padding.
Same page with padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom, 16px) on the fixed navigation.
A full-screen video player that intentionally ignores safe areas for immersion.
